narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 37, slightly edging out the average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many aspire to. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of students in the dataset at over 92%. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your probability of failing the bar at 45.9%, tipping the scales toward rejection of your law school application. This threshold reflects the school's cautious approach to ensuring high bar passage rates, prioritizing applicants likely to pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which signaled potential challenges in sustaining performance over time. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your strong undergraduate GPA, which strongly pulled the prediction in favor of passing by demonstrating your college-level academic strength. Your LSAT score also worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting bar success due to its relevance as a key predictor of legal reasoning skills. While your early Year 1 decile was similarly low at 1, it played a lesser role here compared to these top three drivers. Overall, your profile stands out positively in GPA and LSAT against typical students, but the sharp drop-off in later high school performance raised red flags about long-term readiness. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking courses or standardized tests to boost your decile rankings, seeking mentorship tailored to Black students navigating law school paths, or gaining practical experience like internships to build a more consistent record. With targeted improvements, especially in sustaining grades, you can shift that prediction and open doors to programs that value your unique strengths.
